summary: Steve Yegge announces Gas City v1.0, an open-source SDK built by Julian Knutsen and Chris Sells that deconstructs Gas Town into composable 'packs' for building custom multi-agent orchestration systems. He argues that dark factories should actually be 'light factories' with full observability, and extends his AI Adoption chart to 11 stages, where engineers become shepherds tending flocks of collaborating agents. The post makes a bold case that Gas City enables companies to replace expensive SaaS tools by building bespoke agent-powered alternatives in-house. Yegge emphasizes that Gas City's git-versioned Dolt database and MEOW stack provide the audit trails and reliability that enterprise deployments demand.
key_points: Gas City decomposes Gas Town into declarative 'packs' — composable building blocks for arbitrary multi-agent topologies backed by git-versioned Dolt databases
Single agents are unreliable by design; you should always deploy at least 2-3 agents per task so they catch each other's hallucinations and mistakes
Dark factories aren't just for writing code — they can automate any business process, from image moderation to infrastructure operations
SaaS extracts money from local economies into Silicon Valley while customers use only 20% of features; agent orchestration makes in-housing viable by rebuilding just what you need
Reliability is a dial: more review rounds, backstops, and guardrails let you tune agent teams to whatever reliability level your use case demands
money_quotes: You should almost never deploy a single-agent pack for a real business process. The reality is that any agent can go temporarily insane, at any time, and make a bad call.
In the very near future, devs will become shepherds, tending flocks of agents which do the ground-level work.
SaaS began life as a way for everyone to get savings through specialization and economy of scale. And it has evolved into an extraction machine that's ideal for almost nobody.
Every dollar of SaaS spent outside the U.S. is extracted from a local economy and moved into California's economy.
Reliability, friends, is a dial. You choose where to set it.
A small team of three to five human engineers running Gas City packs can credibly replace seven-figure SaaS bills.
We know now that hallucinations and false memories and forgetting are baked mathematically into all memory systems; there's no avoiding it.
Most other orchestrators I've seen have been able to be super high-ambition. Many lean heavily into the idea that agents, when put together, can just figure stuff out.
key_insight: Multi-agent orchestration systems with full observability and git-versioned audit trails are the missing infrastructure that makes replacing expensive SaaS with in-house agent teams a practical reality.
